text:
The paper focuses on the development of the institution of coalition government in Slovakia in the period between 2008 and 2021. This is the period of major transformation of the Slovak party system on the background of a relatively stable constitutional framework of government. The period witnessed a transformation from the classical system of rivalry in the party system based on a division into left and right wing parties and towards a more pluralistic system with the rise of anti-political and populist parties. At the same time there is a gradual settlement of rules in line with the so-called Ministerial Government Model. Although the basic spirit of this model is associated with the German parliamentary tradition, the content of its individual constitutional elements may be quite different in various countries. In the context of Slovakia, we see the individual constitutive elements emerging in the context of a highly unstable party political framework, which enables great individual freedom for ministers and political parties in the government. This is due to an abolition of the system of crossparty parliamentary watchdogs, the previous system of collective responsibility of members of the coalition council, and the inability of the majority to use it as a means of solving intra-governmental conflicts.

We report on the evolution of executive bargaining and its stability over the period following our previous study. The pace of executive bargaining has not changed substantially and the parties have continued to organize themselves within the office. Yet, the stability of these arrangements has been clearly tested by the string of external shocks including the murder of journalist Ján Kuciak in February 2018 and the COVID-19 pandemic. Under the leadership of the new prime minister, Igor Matovič, the management of the pandemic initially caused a brief shift towards the Dominant Prime Minister Model. We argue that despite Slovakia avoiding substantial cases of democratic regression, the weaknesses of coalition's mutual control have over time led to potential instances of state capture and grand corruption. These trends, tied to high levels of party system personalisation and the inherent organisational fragilities of new parties, strongly undermine predictability of the Slovak coalition pattern in the long term.
